title: Decouple, Collaborate, Match: Prototype-Driven Cognitive Mutual Learning for Brain Tumor Segmentation
authors: Wujie Zhou, Yu Shi, Meixin Fang, Guanghui Yue
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Multimodal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-based brain tumor segmentation faces two critical challenges: (i) insufficient exploitation of inherent frequency-domain characteristics of MRI signals, which leads to the loss of boundary information, and (ii) the difficulty of balancing local morphological adaptability with global semantic consistency in heterogeneous tumors. To address these issues, we propose a collaborative cognitive segmentation network (CCSNet), a mutual learning framework that enhances the performance of two independent networks: the wavelet fusion network (WFNet) and the adaptive neural attention network (ANANet). WFNet operates in the frequency domain, reconstructing tumor boundaries via multilevel wavelet transforms and tumor-centered radial sampling to explicitly capture high-frequency components. In contrast, ANANet operates in the spatial domain, achieving precise semantic modeling of complex tumor morphologies through adaptive region attention and axial kernel adjustments. To further enhance performance, we introduce a mutual learning mechanism comprising two key components: category-aware prototype matching for addressing intraclass heterogeneity and boundary-aware mutual supervision for enforcing consistency constraints via uncertainty-guided region selection. This mechanism enables bidirectional knowledge transfer between WFNet-ML and ANANet-ML, which are derived from WFNet and ANANet, respectively. Extensive experiments on BraTS 2020 and BraTS 2021 datasets demonstrate that the proposed mutual learning framework consistently improves the performance of both networks. Compared with current state-of-the-art methods, CCSNet reduces the 95th percentile Hausdorff Distance metric by 3.00% and 5.14%, respectively, significantly improving tumor boundary localization.

title: Review on Magnetic Field Analytical Methods for Permanent Magnet Machines
authors: Jingyan Chen, Kan Liu, Dong Wei, Jinya Chen, Liang Han, Dinghua Zhang, Yue Liu
abstract: This paper systematically reviews magnetic field analytical methods, categorizing three conventional and three hybrid methods. The accurate modeling of regular shapes can be achieved by the Subdomain method (SD) but suffers from complex multi-layer topologies. The Conformal mapping based method (CM) effectively analyzes complex air gap geometries but exhibits limitations in directly solving inherent 3D spatial effects and nonlinear problems. The Magnetic equivalent circuits method (MEC) is computationally efficient but provides limited accuracy for localized complex magnetic fields. To address nonlinear saturation and topological challenges in radial and axial flux permanent magnet machines, this paper introduces hybrid methods leveraging complementary advantages. The SD-MEC hybrid method is capable of addressing irregular structures but increases computational complexity. The MEC-CM hybrid method offers higher accuracy but exhibits limited adaptability to irregularities. The SD-CM hybrid method has high computational efficiency but is limited in handling nonlinear problems. Therefore, it is imperative for future research on analytical methods to fully account for severe localized saturation, spatial 3D effects, and complex topologies within specialized magnetic regions of PMSMs.

title: Magnetic Induction-Based Device-Free Localization in Underground Endogenous Environment
authors: Kun Chai, Yu Zhang, Shi Cheng, Lixia Xiao, Tao Jiang
abstract: As an important component of the Internet of Things (IoTs), underground localization based on magnetic induction (MI) communication with the received signal strength indicator (RSSI) has been a topic of considerable interest in wireless underground sensor networks (WUSNs). Existing works have widely exploited the active device localization based on wireless communications in underground space, where the transmission media remains air. However, achieving device-free localization (DFL) in an underground endogenous environment presents relatively large gaps. In this paper, we propose a novel MI-based communication method to achieve the DFL accuracy in complex underground endogenous environment. First, we establish an end-to-end MI link model to precisely characterize the RSSI and explicitly correlate MI antenna configurations with magnetic field attenuation in the underground endogenous environment. Subsequently, based on the magnetic-field propagation characteristics, we analyze the shadowing loss caused by the passive target in the MI link. A MI-DFL method is then formulated by incorporating the shadowing model. Finally, extensive numerical simulations across various environmental settings and target types demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method, indicating that MI-DFL attains high localization accuracy within the underground endogenous environments.

title: A Review of Neuro-Symbolic AI for Smart Cities: Architectures, Ontologies, and IoT-Driven Applications
authors: Antonios Pliatsios, Panagiotis Mpatos, Michael Dossis
abstract: Smart cities increasingly rely on Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems, cyber-physical infrastructures, and Artificial Intelligence (AI) to manage transportation, energy distribution, environmental sustainability, public safety, and governance. Deep learning techniques provide high predictive accuracy but often lack transparency, explainability, and semantic awareness. These limitations create major concerns in safety-critical urban environments where legal, ethical, and physical constraints must be respected. Neuro-symbolic Artificial Intelligence (NeSy AI) has emerged as a hybrid paradigm combining neural learning with symbolic reasoning, ontologies, and logical inference. By integrating data-driven perception with structured knowledge representation, neuro-symbolic systems offer interpretable and trustworthy urban intelligence. This review investigates state-of-the-art neuro-symbolic AI applications in smart city ecosystems. Following a structured literature selection process, it examines works published between 2020 and 2026 across major scientific databases. Neuro-symbolic architectures are categorized into sequential pipelines, differentiable reasoning models, and knowledge graph-driven frameworks. Furthermore, applications are analyzed across transportation, smart grids, environmental monitoring, and urban governance. The review highlights ontology integration strategies, explainable AI mechanisms, edge intelligence considerations, and computational limitations. To directly address the identified scalability and semantic drift bottlenecks, this paper proposes a novel, unified Edge-to-Cloud conceptual framework. Finally, future research directions are outlined toward cognitive urban infrastructures capable of explainable and semantically grounded decisionmaking.

title: Unified Machine Learning Framework for Process–Device–Circuit Variability Quantification of Inner Spacer Etch in GAA FETs
authors: Om Maheshwari, Nihar Ranjan Mohapatra
abstract: The inner spacer etch (ISE) process is a critical step in gate-all-around (GAA) nanosheet FET (NsFET) fabrication that directly defines gate length, spacer thickness, and gate-to-source/drain overlap. At advanced nodes, small ISE variations can induce measurable electrical and circuit-level performance shifts, yet their impact beyond geometric deviations remains insufficiently quantified. This work presents a machine-learning driven framework that unifies process, device, and circuit analysis, enabling rapid and quantitative mapping of ISE induced variations to electrical and circuit-level performance for systematic variability quantification. The study identifies leakage current and parasitic capacitance as the most sensitive electrical parameters to ISE fluctuations. Circuit-level analysis further reveals up to 500 MHz deviation in ring oscillator frequency. By enabling fast and quantitative correlation across process, device, and circuit domains, this framework provides a scalable foundation for variability aware near-real-time design technology co-optimization (DTCO) in advanced technologies.

title: HPHF-MMDet: Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection
authors: Guohua Lv, Zhengyang Zhang, Guangxiao Ma, Yongbiao Gao, Jihao Jin, Qian Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Multimodal object detection aims to leverage the complementary characteristics of visible (RGB) and infrared (IR) images to address perception challenges in complex environments. However, existing methods typically utilize structurally uniform backbones that neglect the intrinsic disparities in imaging mechanisms between RGB and IR modalities. Moreover, existing fusion methods fail to effectively address the feature distribution discrepancies across heterogeneous modalities. To address these issues, we propose Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection (HPHF-MMDet). We devise a Modality-Specific Heterogeneous Backbone (MSHB) that diverges from the conventional symmetric feature extraction paradigm. Specifically, for the RGB modality, we employ a Context-Guided Detail Enhancement Module (CGDEM) to dynamically modulate detailed features, suppressing background interference while highlighting target textures. For the IR modality, a Multi-Scale Windmill Convolution (MSWC) is carefully designed to exploit asymmetric receptive fields for capturing the morphological features of thermal targets. Furthermore, to bridge the feature distribution discrepancies between heterogeneous modalities, we introduce a Cross-Modal Complementary Hyperspherical Fusion (CM-CHF) mechanism, which aggregates complementary information through bidirectional hyperspherical alignment. We conduct extensive experiments on the LLVIP, M3FD, and MFAD datasets. Our method achieves mAP50 of 97.9%, 89.8%, and 80.8% respectively, significantly outperforming existing state-of-the-art approaches. The source code is available at https://github.com/zzyadad/HPHF-MMDet.

title: A Multi-Disturbance Resilient CMOS BGR With 3 ppm/°C Drift, 0–10 μ F Load Stability, and 120-dB PSRR
authors: Haitao Cui, Jianwei Zhao, Jiafeng Mao, Xinyue Zheng, Diyang Wu, Weibo Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a multi-disturbance resilient CMOS bandgap reference (BGR) that achieves high precision and robust stability across a wide range of capacitive loads. To overcome the fundamental accuracy bottlenecks of high-precision bandgap references, the proposed architecture systematically addresses four major error sources: temperature drift, power supply fluctuations, internal circuit noise, and output load disturbances. A high-order curvature compensation network is employed to substantially eliminate nonlinear base-emitter voltage variations, achieving an ultra-low temperature coefficient. A three-stage chopper-stabilized operational amplifier is utilized to effectively attenuate low-frequency flicker noise and minimize offset drift. To suppress external supply noise, a pre-regulated low-dropout (LDO) regulator is integrated to isolate the sensitive bandgap core. At the output stage, a nested source-feedback (NSF) buffer topology is proposed to actively reduce the open-loop output impedance, ensuring absolute stability across a wide capacitive load range of 0 to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>F. Fabricated in a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.18~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m CMOS process, the BGR occupies a total area of 0.8 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Measurement results confirm that, operating with a quiescent current of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$150~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>A, the proposed design achieves a maximum temperature drift of only 3ppm/°C over a temperature range of −40°C to 125°C after single-point calibration. Additionally, the BGR delivers a high power-supply rejection ratio (PSRR) of 120dB at 10Hz, and limits the 0.1 to 10Hz integrated noise to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.5~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>Vrms.

title: Low-Frequency Magnetic Induction Tomography for Superparamagnetic Iron Oxide Nanoparticles based Stroke Imaging
authors: Gui Jin, Chuanqian Xia, Feng Wang, Lian Yan, Chen Zhou, Siqiao Liu, Yutong Wu, Lin Xu, Jia Xu, Ke Ma, Jian Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Stroke is a leading cause of mortality and disability worldwide, with rapid diagnosis critical for effective intervention. Conventional imaging modalities such as CT and MRI are limited by their size, cost, and unsuitability for bedside or prehospital emergency use. This study presents a novel Superparamagnetic Iron Oxide Nanoparticles (SPIOs) based Magnetic Induction Tomography (SPIO-MIT) for non-invasive stroke-tracing imaging. Operating at an ultra-low excitation frequency of 3 kHz and a magnetic field strength below 1 mT, the system effectively suppresses background interference from biological tissues while enhancing sensitivity to SPIOs distribution. System performance was validated through phantom experiments featuring varied SPIOs positions, geometries, and concentration gradients, confirming accurate imaging of SPIOs with negligible response to low-conductivity substances. Complementary in vitro experiments embedding SPIO-blood mixtures in biological tissues demonstrated minimal background interference. In vivo experiments on a rabbit intracerebral hemorrhage model further confirmed precise localization of SPIOs injected into the brain, with detection sensitivity down to 1.2 mg of iron and an imaging diameter of 60 mm. In summary, the developed SPIO-MIT system enables sensitive, non-invasive spatial mapping of SPIOs. By combining the high sensitivity of magnetic particle imaging with the operational simplicity and biological safety of magnetic induction tomography, SPIO-MIT represents a promising technique for portable, bedside, and prehospital stroke diagnostics.

authors: Wenhai Lai, Mingxiao Li, Kaiming Shen, Liyao Xiang, Zhi-Quan Luo

title: Relative Depth Guided Cross Domain Semantic Segmentation for High Resolution Satellite Remote Sensing Images
authors: Yongqi Sun, Yujun Quan, Anzhu Yu, Yu Su, Xin Li, Chenguang Dai, Xuanguang Liu, Yanfei Zhong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Satellite remote sensing images exhibit significant cross-domain discrepancies due to variations in sensors, imaging conditions, and regional characteristics. These discrepancies degrade the performance of semantic segmentation models on unseen target domains. Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) mitigates this problem but requires target domain images during training. Achieving robust cross-domain generalization with foundation models typically relies on large-scale architectures, incurring substantial computational overhead that limits practical deployment. This paper introduces a novel perspective by leveraging zero-shot relative depth maps as domain-invariant geometric priors to guide cross-domain semantic segmentation of satellite remote sensing images. The proposed framework is compatible with most encoder-decoder architectures and makes three key contributions: 1) A unified depth-guided framework for optical remote sensing cross-domain segmentation. 2) A multi-scale contrastive learning module that aligns optical and relative depth features, enhancing optical feature representation. 3) A refinement strategy using shallow relative depth features to guide semantic mask prediction. Experiments across three cross-domain scenarios, including the LoveDA benchmark, a self-constructed circumpolar cross-seasonal dataset, and a cross=-dataset generalization setting, demonstrate that relative depth serves as an effective spatial prior. Notably, the proposed method achieves comparable or superior accuracy to state-of-the-art UDA approaches that require target-domain images during training, while operating under the stricter zero-shot domain generalization setting where no target data is accessible. The codes will be released at https://github.com/ArthasMil/DGCD.

title: Infrared Small Target Detection via Graph Fourier Transform Tensor Nuclear Norm and Spatio-Temporal Overlapping Group Sparsity
authors: Anqing Wu, Qiang Guo, Mykola Kaliuzhnyi, Yupeng Chen, Na Fu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Infrared small target detection in heterogeneous and dynamic scenes remains a challenging problem due to the lack of intrinsic target features and the complexity of background clutter. Although Tensor Robust Principal Component Analysis (TRPCA) methods have achieved significant progress, they typically rely on the singular value decomposition (SVD) based tensor nuclear norm (TNN), which assumes linear correlations across temporal frames. This assumption often fails in highly dynamic scenes (e.g., rapid cloud movement or sensor jitter) where background changes are non-linear. To address this limitation, this article proposes a novel method combining Graph Fourier Transform-based Tensor Nuclear Norm (GFT-TNN) and Spatio-Temporal Overlapping Group Sparsity (ST-OGS). First, to capture the intrinsic manifold structure of dynamic backgrounds, we construct a temporal graph that encodes the non-linear relationships between frames. A GFT-TNN is then introduced to constrain the background tensor in the graph spectral domain, offering a more robust low-rank representation than traditional SVD-based approaches. Second, recognizing that targets exhibit spatio-temporal continuity rather than appearing as isolated pixels, we employ an ST-OGS regularization. This imposes a 3D structural constraint on the target tensor, effectively distinguishing true targets from random, high-intensity sensor noise. Finally, to solve the proposed model effectively, we develop an optimization algorithm based on the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM). Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method yields superior Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and background suppression capabilities compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

title: Knowledge-aided SAR Jamming Imaging: Algorithm-Dependent Mechanism and Characterization
authors: Jiawei Tao, Ying Zhang, Mengmeng Li, Dazhi Ding, Henry Leung, Maria Sabrina Greco
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate characterization of algorithm-dependent jamming effects enables optimized synthetic aperture radar (SAR) system design with inherent anti-jamming capabilities, particularly through imaging mechanism selection. Traditional jamming approaches fail to account for the specific characteristics of SAR imaging and typically validate jamming effectiveness using single look complex (SLC) data. This methodology inadequately addresses the practical demands of anti-jamming, as the migration compensation mechanisms embedded in the SAR imaging processing chain may change the characteristics of jamming signals, where both the spectrum support and focusing performance critically affect the jamming effects. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time that a theoretical mechanism between active jamming and SAR imaging is established. Based on the polar format mechanism (PFM) and wavenumber domain mechanism (WDM), rigorous mathematical derivations are conducted to characterize the imaging processing chain of representative barrage and deception jamming techniques. Furthermore, residual range cell migration (RCM) and high-order phase error characteristics under PFM and WDM are analyzed. Experimental results indicate that WDM retains larger RCM with small high-order phase errors, whereas PFM mitigates RCM but induces azimuth defocusing due to uncorrected high-order phase errors. Through theoretical analysis and experimental verification using five representative jamming types, we characterize the algorithm-dependent jamming manifestations in SAR imaging chains, providing quantitative support for jamming recognition and the evaluation of SAR anti-jamming performance.

title: Physics Informed Fuzzy Extended State Observer-based Active Adaptive Control for Flapping-Wing Micro Aerial Vehicles
authors: Xiaogang Li, Vu Phi Tran, Sreenatha Annavati, Matthew Garratt
abstract: Flapping-wing micro aerial vehicles (FWMAVs) exhibit strong nonlinear coupling, underactuation, and high sensitivity to unsteady aerodynamic disturbances, which makes precise trajectory tracking challenging. This paper proposes an actively adaptive control framework, termed AA-BFBEL+PI-FESO, that integrates an Adaptive Bidirectional Fuzzy Brain Emotional Learning controller with a Physics-Informed Fuzzy Extended State Observer. The controller provides online emotional-parameter adaptation through a gradient-based update law with Lyapunov-guaranteed stability, while the observer incorporates partially known aerodynamic information to improve disturbance estimation accuracy. A shared fuzzy inference layer further reduces computational redundancy. Comparative simulations show that the proposed method significantly outperforms PID, SMC, ADRC, and conventional BFBEL controllers, reducing RMSE, maximum tracking error, overshoot, settling time, and ITAE by up to 58.7%. In addition, relative computational analysis indicates reductions of 68.4% in execution time, 58.1% in memory usage, and 52.5% in CPU load. Hardware-in-the-loop experiments on the STM32F405 flight-control processor further confirm the real-time feasibility of the proposed framework.
